title: Network Hubs
description: Legacy Layer 1 devices that broadcast all incoming traffic to every port — replaced by switches.
fullExplanation: A hub is a multiport repeater. When a frame arrives on any port, the hub broadcasts it out all other ports. Every device receives every frame — devices must check the destination MAC themselves and discard frames not addressed to them. All ports share a single collision domain — only one device can transmit at a time (CSMA/CD). Hubs are obsolete — switches replaced them because switches forward frames only to the correct port, eliminating collisions and dramatically improving performance.

hubVsSwitch.switch: Forwards to specific port via MAC table
Each port is its own collision domain

keyFacts: Hubs create a single collision domain — only one device can transmit at a time
CSMA/CD: devices listen before transmitting, back off on collision
All devices on a hub share total bandwidth (e.g. 10Mbps shared among 8 ports)
Promiscuous mode NIC on a hub sees ALL traffic — easy to sniff
Hubs are obsolete — last manufactured in early 2000s
Managed switches can mirror ports (SPAN) to replicate hub behavior for analysis
Passive hub: just wires. Active hub: regenerates signal (repeater).
